question: Tell me about a time you solved a difficult technical problem.
situation: Our production recommendation service started returning stale results hours after a deployment.
task: I was on-call and needed to diagnose and fix the issue before the morning business peak.
action: I traced logs to a misconfigured cache TTL introduced in the new deployment — it was set to 24h instead of 5m. I rolled back the config, wrote a hotfix with the correct value, and added a validation test to the deployment pipeline.
result: Service recovered within 20 minutes. The pipeline guard prevented the same class of error from recurring. Added to post-mortem documentation.
follow_up: How did you communicate the incident to stakeholders?

question: Describe a situation where you had to collaborate with a difficult teammate.
situation: A senior developer on my team consistently dismissed junior members' ideas in code reviews, causing low morale.
task: As an informal team lead, I needed to address this without escalating to management prematurely.
action: I had a private 1-on-1 with them, acknowledged their expertise, and explained the impact of their communication style. I suggested a structured review checklist so feedback stayed objective.
result: The teammate adopted the checklist. Review cycles became more constructive. Two junior members who had been disengaged became active contributors within a month.
follow_up: What would you have done if the behaviour hadn't changed?

question: Tell me about a time you led a project under tight deadlines.
situation: Our team was asked to integrate a third-party payment API in 5 days before a product launch.
task: I was assigned tech lead for the integration despite the team being unfamiliar with the API.
action: I divided work into parallel tracks — one person on API auth, one on webhook handling, one on error states. I ran daily 15-minute standups and unblocked blockers in real time. I also negotiated with product to defer non-critical error scenarios to a follow-up sprint.
result: Integration shipped on day 4. Launch happened on schedule. The deferred items were completed in the next sprint with zero customer impact.
follow_up: How did you prioritise which error scenarios to defer?

question: Give an example of a time you disagreed with your manager.
situation: My manager wanted to use a NoSQL database for a feature that I believed needed strong relational integrity.
task: I needed to either align with the decision or make a compelling technical case for an alternative.
action: I prepared a short written comparison — schema complexity, query patterns, data consistency requirements — and requested 20 minutes to walk through it. I acknowledged the valid reasons for NoSQL (scalability) and proposed a hybrid approach: relational for the transactional core, NoSQL for the activity feed.
result: Manager approved the hybrid design. The relational core prevented three categories of data inconsistency bugs identified during QA. Manager later cited it as the right call in a team retrospective.
follow_up: What would you have done if your manager still disagreed after your case?

question: Tell me about a time you had to learn a new technology quickly.
situation: Mid-project, the client switched the required cloud platform from AWS to IBM Cloud with two weeks to go.
task: I needed to migrate our deployment setup and learn IBM Cloud services without delaying delivery.
action: I spent the first two days on IBM Cloud documentation and completed the relevant learning path on IBM Skills. I identified service equivalences (S3→COS, Lambda→IBM Functions) and created a migration checklist. I paired with a teammate to validate each step.
result: Migration completed in 8 days — 6 days ahead of the deadline. I also documented the process for the team, which became a reusable runbook.
follow_up: What was the hardest part of the switch?

question: Describe a time you improved a process without being asked.
situation: Our team's manual QA regression suite took 3 hours per release, blocking deployments.
task: There was no assigned owner for test automation, but the bottleneck was affecting the whole team.
action: I volunteered to automate the top 20 highest-risk test cases using Playwright in my spare time over two weeks. I got informal buy-in from my manager, demoed after the first 10 tests were done, and got official sprint time allocated for the remainder.
result: Regression suite time dropped from 3 hours to 18 minutes. Deployment frequency increased from weekly to twice-weekly. The test suite became the team standard.
follow_up: How did you decide which test cases to automate first?

question: Tell me about a time you explained a complex technical concept to a non-technical audience.
situation: Stakeholders were pushing back on a database migration project because they didn't understand the risk of not doing it.
task: I needed to explain technical debt and legacy system risk to a VP with no engineering background.
action: I created a one-page visual analogy comparing our codebase to a building with a crumbling foundation — safe today but risky to extend. I quantified the risk: three production incidents in the past quarter traced to the legacy schema. I avoided jargon and focused on business impact.
result: VP approved the migration budget in the same meeting. The project was completed the following quarter, eliminating that class of incidents.
follow_up: How do you adjust your communication style for different audiences?

question: Tell me about a time you failed and what you learned.
situation: I underestimated the complexity of a feature and committed to a deadline I couldn't meet.
task: I had to manage the fallout — both technically completing the work and communicating the delay to the team.
action: As soon as I recognised the gap (day 3 of 5), I immediately told my manager rather than waiting. I presented a revised timeline with justification and offered to descope lower-priority sub-features. I worked extra hours to minimise the delay.
result: Feature shipped 2 days late. Customer impact was minimal due to early communication and partial release. I now break tasks into daily checkpoints and flag risk at the first sign, not the last.
follow_up: How has this changed how you estimate tasks today?

question: Describe a time you had to manage multiple priorities simultaneously.
situation: I was simultaneously handling a production bug, a feature deadline, and onboarding a new team member.
task: All three were time-sensitive — I needed to triage without dropping any.
action: I triaged by impact: production bug first (customer-facing). I timeboxed the bug investigation to 90 minutes with a clear escalation path if unsolved. Delegated an onboarding task to a senior teammate for one day. Communicated the feature timeline risk to the PM with a revised date.
result: Bug fixed in 70 minutes. New member onboarded on schedule. Feature delivered one day late with PM agreement. No customer escalation.
follow_up: What frameworks or tools do you use to manage your priorities?

question: Tell me about a time you went beyond expectations to help a user.
situation: A key client reported that our data export feature was generating corrupted CSVs affecting their downstream pipeline.
task: It was reported at 5pm on a Friday — outside normal support hours.
action: I volunteered to investigate immediately. Root cause: Unicode characters in one column were breaking the delimiter. I fixed the encoding logic, wrote a migration script for previously corrupted exports, and sent the client a corrected file set with an explanation and apology.
result: Client replied the same evening, grateful for the speed. They expanded their contract the following quarter, citing our support responsiveness as a key reason.
follow_up: How do you balance urgency for individual clients against team workload?
